section.title: The Passover
section.sectionRationale: This is the founding ordinance of Israel's national life, instituted on the very night of deliverance and commanded to be kept forever. It gives Israel, and the whole Bible after it, its central picture of judgment averted by a substitute's blood.
section.thesis: The LORD institutes the Passover: a spotless lamb slain in the place of the firstborn, its blood marking the door so that judgment passes over, to be kept as a perpetual memorial of redemption.
section.frameNote: Four movements: instructions for choosing and eating the lamb; the promise that the blood will avert judgment; the memorial feast for the generations to come; and Moses relaying it all to the elders, who believe and obey.

units.label: Instructions for the lamb
units.note: a new calendar, a chosen lamb

media.alt: A spotless lamb and blood on a doorframe
media.caption: A lamb without blemish, its blood marking the door.

verses.text: Now the LORD said to Moses and Aaron in the land of Egypt,
“This month is the beginning of months for you; it shall be the first month of your year.
Tell the whole congregation of Israel that on the tenth day of this month each man must select a lamb for his family, one per household.
If the household is too small for a whole lamb, they are to share with the nearest neighbor based on the number of people, and apportion the lamb accordingly.
Your lamb must be an {{unblemished|unblemished}} year-old male, and you may take it from the sheep or the goats.
You must keep it until the fourteenth day of the month, when the whole assembly of the congregation of Israel will slaughter the animals at twilight.
They are to take some of the {{blood|blood}} and put it on the sides and tops of the doorframes of the houses where they eat the lambs.
They are to eat the meat that night, roasted over the fire, along with unleavened bread and bitter herbs.
Do not eat any of the meat raw or cooked in boiling water, but only roasted over the fire, its head and legs and inner parts.
Do not leave any of it until morning; before the morning you must burn up any part that is left over.
This is how you are to eat it: You must be fully dressed for travel, with your sandals on your feet and your staff in your hand. You are to eat in haste; it is the LORD's {{passover|Passover}}.

units.label: The judgment and the sign of blood
units.note: the blood shall be a sign, and I will pass over you

verses.text: On that night I will pass through the land of Egypt and strike down every firstborn male, both man and beast, and I will execute judgment against all the gods of Egypt. I am the LORD.
The blood on the houses where you are staying will distinguish them; when I see the blood, I will pass over you. No plague will fall on you to destroy you when I strike the land of Egypt.
And this day will be a memorial for you, and you are to celebrate it as a feast to the LORD, as a permanent statute for the generations to come.

For seven days you must eat unleavened bread. On the first day you are to remove the leaven from your houses. Whoever eats anything leavened from the first day through the seventh must be cut off from Israel.
On the first day you are to hold a sacred assembly, and another on the seventh day. You must not do any work on those days, except to prepare the meals, that is all you may do.
So you are to keep the Feast of Unleavened Bread, for on this very day I brought your divisions out of the land of Egypt. You must keep this day as a permanent statute for the generations to come.
In the first month you are to eat unleavened bread, from the evening of the fourteenth day until the evening of the twenty-first day.

For seven days there must be no leaven found in your houses. If anyone eats something leavened, that person, whether a foreigner or native of the land, must be cut off from the congregation of Israel.
You are not to eat anything leavened; eat unleavened bread in all your homes.”
Then Moses summoned all the elders of Israel and told them, “Go at once and select for yourselves a lamb for each family, and slaughter the Passover lamb.
Take a cluster of hyssop, dip it into the blood in the basin, and brush the blood on the top and sides of the doorframe. None of you shall go out the door of his house until morning.

When the LORD passes through to strike down the Egyptians, He will see the blood on the top and sides of the doorframe and will pass over that doorway; so He will not allow the destroyer to enter your houses and strike you down.
And you are to keep this command as a permanent statute for you and your descendants.
When you enter the land that the LORD will give you as He promised, you are to keep this service.
When your children ask you, ‘What does this service mean to you?’

you are to reply, ‘It is the Passover sacrifice to the LORD, who passed over the houses of the Israelites in Egypt when He struck down the Egyptians and spared our homes.’” 
Then the people bowed down and worshiped.
And the Israelites went and did just what the LORD had commanded Moses and Aaron.

unblemished.gloss: Without defect, whole, complete. The lamb must be flawless, fit to stand in another's place; the New Testament calls Christ a lamb without blemish or defect, 1 Peter 1:19.

blood.gloss: Blood, the life poured out. It is not the lamb's death alone that saves but the blood applied, visibly marking the house; the same principle runs through the whole sacrificial system and into the cross.

passover.gloss: To pass over, to spring over, to spare. The word names both the act, God passing over the marked houses, and the festival that remembers it, and gives its name to Christ, our Passover lamb, 1 Corinthians 5:7.

basic.context: On the very last night in Egypt, God gives Israel a new calendar and a new meal. Each family takes a spotless lamb, kills it, and paints its blood on the doorframe. That night, judgment falls on Egypt's firstborn, but wherever the blood is seen, the LORD passes over the house.
basic.hermeneutics: This really happened, on a specific night, and Israel was commanded to keep it every year after. It teaches an unchanging truth: judgment is real, and only a substitute's blood, not personal goodness, turns it aside. Christians do not keep the Passover meal itself, but the substance it pictured is fulfilled in Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: The lamb must be tamim, without blemish, a flawless substitute. What saves is the dam, the blood, applied and seen, not simply the animal's death in the abstract. And the name pesach, Passover, describes exactly what happens: the LORD springs over, passes over, the marked house.
basic.typology: This is the Bible's clearest picture of substitutionary atonement before the cross: an unblemished lamb dies so the firstborn lives, and its blood, applied by faith, is what saves. Paul calls Christ our Passover lamb outright; John's Gospel deliberately echoes Passover details in Jesus' death.
basic.christ: 1 Corinthians 5:7 names Jesus directly as our Passover lamb, sacrificed once for all. John notes that none of Jesus' bones were broken, fulfilling the Passover rule in Exodus 12:46, and John the Baptist calls Him the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world.
basic.god: God is the one who judges Egypt, yet He is also the one who provides the way of escape before He strikes. His justice and His mercy meet exactly at the doorframe: judgment is real and falls, but a substitute has already borne it for those who trust and obey.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 5:7 calls Christ our Passover lamb. John 19:36 sees the unbroken bones fulfilled at the cross. John 1:29 calls Jesus the Lamb of God. 1 Peter 1:18-19 speaks of redemption by the precious blood of a lamb without blemish or defect.
basic.oneStory: This is the night Israel learns the shape of salvation: not escape by good behavior, but rescue through a substitute's blood, applied in faith and obedience. Every Passover after this one points forward to the true Lamb, until Christ dies at Passover itself and becomes what this night only pictured.

context.literary: The chapter moves from precise ceremonial instruction, choosing, keeping, and eating the lamb, to the theological center in verses 12 and 13, then to the memorial statute for future generations, closing with Moses relaying it to the elders who obey immediately.
context.historical: This becomes the founding date of Israel's calendar and its central annual feast, observed continuously through the Old Testament and still kept by Jewish people today; it is the meal Jesus shares with His disciples on the night He is betrayed.
context.canonical: The Passover becomes the Bible's primary lens for understanding deliverance through a substitute's blood, feeding directly into the sacrificial system, the prophets' language of redemption, and the New Testament's description of the cross.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish, at the founding moment of Israel's national existence, that deliverance from judgment comes only through a spotless substitute's blood, applied by faith and obedience, and to be remembered forever.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage prescribes a specific Old Testament ordinance for Israel, not a rite for the church; the New Testament treats it as fulfilled rather than repeated, with Christ Himself as the once-for-all Passover lamb, and the Lord's Supper as the meal that now looks back to His finished work.

debates.question: Should Christians keep a Passover meal today?

views.label: Fulfilled, not repeated
views.case: Since Christ is our Passover lamb, sacrificed once for all, 1 Corinthians 5:7, the shadow gives way to the substance; the church's meal is the Lord's Supper, not a re-enactment of the Exodus Passover.

views.label: Instructive remembrance
views.case: Some Christians value a Christian seder as a teaching tool to appreciate the Jewish roots of the gospel, while still affirming that no ongoing ceremonial obligation binds believers under the new covenant.

debates.bearing: Both views agree Passover finds its true fulfillment in Christ; the question is only whether its form remains useful pedagogically, not whether it remains obligatory.

study.originalLanguages: The lamb must be tamim, without blemish, fit to be a worthy substitute. Salvation comes through the dam, the blood, actually applied to the doorframe, not merely shed. And the whole night takes its name from pesach, the LORD passing over, springing over, the marked houses in judgment.

typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Lamb of God without blemish or defect, whose blood alone shields from judgment, John 1:29, 1 Peter 1:19.
The blood of Christ applied by faith, by which the believer is passed over in the final judgment, Romans 3:25, 1 Corinthians 5:7.

typology.type: Not one bone of the lamb broken
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled literally at the cross, where the soldiers did not break Jesus' legs, John 19:33-36.

study.christ: Paul states it plainly: Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed, 1 Corinthians 5:7. John's Passion narrative deliberately echoes this chapter, from the timing of Jesus' death at Passover to the unbroken bones foretold in verse 46 of this very chapter. John the Baptist's first words about Jesus, behold the Lamb of God, gather up this whole scene and point it at one man.
study.god: God is both Judge and Redeemer in the same night. He does not overlook Egypt's sin, nor does He simply spare Israel because they are His people apart from any provision; He supplies the substitute Himself and requires that its blood be applied. Mercy does not cancel justice here; it satisfies it through a lamb.

crossReferences.note: Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed once for all.
Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world.
The soldiers did not break Jesus' legs, fulfilling this chapter's rule about the Passover lamb's bones.
Redeemed not with silver or gold but with the precious blood of a lamb without blemish or defect.

study.oneStory: At the founding moment of Israel's life as a people, God teaches the pattern the whole Bible will follow: judgment is real, but a spotless substitute's blood, applied in faith, turns it aside. Every Passover kept after this one waits for the true Lamb, until Jesus dies at Passover and becomes, once for all, what this night only pictured.

sermon.title: The Story of the Lamb
sermon.why: Preached on Exodus 12:1-13, Keller works through the Passover lamb as the pattern the whole Bible uses to explain substitutionary atonement, tracing it directly to Christ as the true Lamb of God.

quiz.q: What kind of lamb did each household have to select?

options.text: An unblemished, year-old male, from the sheep or the goats.
options.note: Verse 5 gives this exact requirement, a flawless substitute.

options.text: Any lamb available, blemished or not.
options.note: The text is specific: it must be without blemish, not just any lamb.

options.text: A lamb over three years old.
options.note: The requirement is a year-old male, not an older animal.

options.text: No animal at all was required.
options.note: An animal sacrifice, a lamb or goat, was explicitly required.

quiz.q: What made the difference between judgment and safety on that night?

options.text: The blood applied visibly to the doorframe of the house.
options.note: Verses 13 and 23 make this the deciding sign, seen by the LORD Himself.

options.text: How sincerely the family prayed that night.
options.note: Sincerity mattered, but the text points to the visible blood as the actual sign of safety.

options.text: Whether the family was wealthy enough to buy a large lamb.
options.note: Poorer households could share a lamb; wealth was never the deciding factor.

options.text: Nothing distinguished Israelite and Egyptian homes.
options.note: The text is explicit that the blood made a clear, life-saving distinction.

quiz.q: How were the Israelites to eat the Passover meal, according to verse 11?

options.text: Fully dressed for travel, sandals on, staff in hand, eating in haste.
options.note: This detail signals urgency, they were about to be driven out that very night.

options.text: Reclining at leisure over several days.
options.note: The instructions stress urgency and readiness to leave, not leisure.

options.text: Fasting completely through the night.
options.note: They were commanded to eat the lamb, not fast from it.

options.text: Standing outside the house in the open street.
options.note: They were to stay inside the house until morning, verse 22.

quiz.q: Why was this day to be kept as a permanent statute?

options.text: As a memorial of the LORD bringing Israel's divisions out of Egypt on that very day.
options.note: Verses 14 and 17 give exactly this reason, a remembrance of redemption.

options.text: Because Pharaoh requested an annual tribute.
options.note: The memorial is commanded by the LORD for Israel's remembrance, not for Pharaoh's benefit.

options.text: So Israel could forget what happened in Egypt.
options.note: The opposite is true; the feast exists precisely so Israel would never forget.

options.text: There is no stated reason for keeping it.
options.note: The text gives an explicit reason, remembering the exodus from Egypt.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament interpret the Passover lamb?

options.text: As a picture fulfilled in Christ, our Passover lamb, sacrificed once for all.
options.note: 1 Corinthians 5:7 states this directly, and John's Gospel echoes Passover details at the cross.

options.text: As a rite Christians are still commanded to repeat every year.
options.note: The New Testament treats it as fulfilled in Christ rather than an ongoing obligation for the church.

options.text: As a story with no connection to Jesus.
options.note: The connections, in Paul, John, and Peter, are explicit and deliberate.

options.text: As a purely political event with no theological meaning.
options.note: The New Testament treats it as deeply theological, the pattern for substitutionary atonement.

questions: The lamb had to be spotless, standing in for a household that was not. What does it mean for you that Christ, the truly spotless one, stood in your place?
The blood had to be applied, seen on the door, not just shed in the yard. Is your trust resting on Christ's finished work actually applied to you by faith, or on some vaguer hope?
Israel ate in haste, dressed for a journey that would begin that very night. Are you living each day as someone ready to be moved by God, or settled and comfortable in Egypt?
The feast was for remembering, told to children for generations. How well do you tell the story of your own rescue in Christ to the people who come after you?
The people bowed down and worshiped, then simply did what they were told. Where is God asking for that same combination from you this week, worship followed by obedience?

translationNotes.note: Twilight renders a Hebrew phrase literally meaning between the two evenings, understood as the late afternoon before sunset, the time appointed for the lambs to be slaughtered.
The LORD's Passover translates pesach, which can mean both the act of passing over in judgment and the festival meal itself; the wordplay is deliberate through the whole chapter.
Cut off from Israel likely describes exclusion from the covenant community, whether by formal expulsion or divine judgment; the severity underlines how seriously God takes this ordinance.
